summary: Adam is a second-year BA student caught between two worlds - his family home in rural Cavan and his makeshift Dublin existence sleeping on a friend's couch. His extreme 4-5 hour daily commute has created a lifestyle entirely dependent on food delivery, ordering 5+ times per week when in Dublin. Despite describing himself as money-conscious and a 'saver,' he spends an extraordinary €140 weekly on food (80% on takeaways), suggesting a disconnect between his financial self-perception and reality. He's strategically subscribed to both Uber One and Deliveroo Plus, constantly price-comparing and promotion-hunting across platforms. His relationship with food is purely functional during weekdays - quick fuel between lectures and commutes - but transforms into social experiences on weekends when he's home with family and friends. There's a palpable tension between his stated love of cooking and the practical impossibility of his current living situation, creating guilt around his delivery dependency that he rationalizes through time constraints.
personality_notes: Articulate and self-aware but somewhat defensive about his habits. Shows genuine enthusiasm when discussing promotions and deals. Tends to rationalize behaviors while simultaneously expressing guilt about them. Honest about contradictions in his lifestyle.
food_relationship: Food delivery serves as his primary survival mechanism during an unsustainable commuting lifestyle, creating dependency masked as convenience.

key_moments.description: Describes his brutal 5am wake-up, 4-5 hour daily commute, and makeshift Dublin accommodation
key_moments.significance: Establishes the extreme lifestyle that drives his delivery dependency - this isn't student laziness but genuine survival strategy

key_moments.description: Reveals spending €140 weekly on food, 80% on takeaways
key_moments.significance: Massive contradiction with his self-described 'saver' identity - suggests lack of awareness of true spending or different definition of value

key_moments.description: Expresses guilt about not tipping drivers and staying home instead of going out for food
key_moments.significance: Shows internal conflict between convenience and values, plus awareness that his behavior doesn't align with his preferences

what_not_said: Never mentioned the financial strain of his lifestyle despite massive food spending
Avoided discussing the sustainability of sleeping on a friend's couch long-term
Didn't acknowledge the health implications of his heavily processed diet
initial_hypotheses: His extreme commute creates genuine time poverty that justifies delivery dependency
Price sensitivity is about per-transaction value rather than total spending awareness
Weekend behavior at home reveals his preferred relationship with food when circumstances allow
Subscription strategy shows sophisticated platform optimization despite claiming to be budget-conscious
questions_for_coding: How do extreme lifestyle circumstances change the moral framework around food choices?
What role do promotions play in justifying otherwise guilt-inducing behaviors?
How does temporary accommodation status affect food decision-making?
What's the relationship between stated values ('I'm a saver') and actual behavior patterns?
diary_summary: 7-day diary shows dramatically different eating patterns when at family home in Cavan - more home cooking, family meals, and only 1 delivery order. Skipped 5 meals across the week, suggesting irregular eating patterns.
say_do_gaps: Claims to enjoy cooking but only cooked 3 times in 7 days when actually at home with kitchen access
Describes himself as money-conscious but spends €140 weekly on food
Says he orders 5+ times per week but diary shows only 1 delivery order (though this was during home week, not Dublin week)
